result.question:
Research question: What does peer-reviewed / scientific research establish about BRANDING — specifically how to DESIGN a brand's visual identity, and how brands work emotionally/psychologically — well enough to codify into an evidence-based "branding" design skill?

CONSTRAINT: Lean ONLY on academic / scientific studies — peer-reviewed journals, controlled experiments, meta-analyses, marketing-science and design-research literature (Journal of Marketing, JMR, Journal of Consumer Research, Journal of Consumer Psychology, Journal of the Academy of Marketing Science, Psychology & Marketing, design journals). EXCLUDE agency blog posts, opinion pieces, and folklore — name the study/author behind every claim. Weight the coverage roughly 70% on brand DESIGN and 30% on branding / emotional psychology.

1) BRAND DESIGN (~70%, the priority) — empirical findings on the visual elements of brand identity and their MEASURED effects:
- LOGO design: descriptive vs non-descriptive, abstract vs concrete, "natural"/harmonious/elaborate dimensions, symmetry, complexity, dynamism/movement/tilt, roundness vs angularity, true-name vs not — and effects on recognition, recall, attitude, perceived authenticity, commitment, willingness-to-pay, brand performance. (e.g. Henderson & Cote 1998; Luffarelli, Mukesh & Mahmood 2019; Luffarelli, Stamatogiannakis & Yang; Hagtvedt 2011 incomplete-logo; Walsh, Winterich & Mittal logo redesign; Pieters & Wedel.)
- Brand COLOUR: colour and brand personality / recognition, single vs multi-colour, colour-meaning associations, colour's role in brand identification and the "increases recognition" claims. (Labrecque & Milne 2012/2013; Bottomley & Doyle; Hynes; Garber.)
- Brand TYPOGRAPHY / typeface: typeface design dimensions and their effect on brand personality and perception; naturalness, congruence, weight. (Henderson, Giese & Cote 2004; Grohmann, Giese & Parkman; Childers & Jass.)
- SHAPE / FORM: angular vs rounded logos/packaging and perceived brand attributes; visual complexity. (Jiang, Gorn, Galli & Chattopadhyay; Zhang, Feick & Price.)
- PACKAGING design, brand-identity systems, logo redesign effects, visual consistency, processing fluency.
- Outcome constructs: brand recognition, recall, brand equity, perceived quality, brand–design congruence/fit, fluency.

2) BRANDING / EMOTIONAL PSYCHOLOGY (~30%) — the psychological mechanisms and how design triggers them:
- Brand personality (Aaker 1997 dimensions and the critiques/replication issues).
- Self–brand connection, brand attachment, brand love, emotional attachment. (Thomson, MacInnis & Park 2005; Batra, Ahuvia & Bagozzi 2012; Park et al.)
- Emotion / affect / arousal in brand response; processing fluency → preference (Reber, Schwarz & Winkielman); brand-as-relationship; experiential branding (Schmitt).

3) SYNTHESIS INTO A METHOD: distil actionable, evidence-ranked principles and a repeatable PROCESS for designing a brand's visual identity grounded in the findings — separating proven findings [STRONG] from established convention [CONVENTION] from myth [MYTH] wherever the evidence allows (e.g. is "colour increases recognition by 80%" a myth? is more logo symmetry always better? does a descriptive logo always win?).

Deliverable: a cited report to codify into an evidence-based "branding" skill for the design-craft plugin (which already ships colour, charting, UX/layout, typography, plus visual-style and developing-style). The branding skill must stay subordinate to those accessibility / legibility / chart-honesty floors.
result.summary: Synthesis step was skipped or failed — returning 15 verified claims unmerged.

confirmed.claim: Henderson & Cote (1998) derived their logo guidelines empirically, not by opinion: they analyzed 195 real logos each rated on 13 design characteristics, then identified which logos satisfy three distinct communication objectives (high-recognition, low-investment, high-image). This is the empirical, codifiable foundation of the 13-dimension logo-design framework.
confirmed.quote: An empirical analysis of 195 logos, calibrated on 13 design characteristics, identified logos that meet high-recognition, low-investment, and high-image communication objectives.

confirmed.claim: For accurate brand recognition, the prescribed design profile is 'very natural, very harmonious, and moderately elaborate' — note the elaborateness optimum is MODERATE, not maximal, which falsifies any 'more complexity/elaboration is always better' rule for recognition.
confirmed.quote: High-recognition logos (accurate recognition created by high investment) should be very natural, very harmonious, and moderately elaborate.

confirmed.claim: The optimal 'natural' level is objective-dependent and can reverse: a logo designed to create a false sense of familiarity and positive affect without heavy media investment should be LESS natural (and very harmonious) — the opposite direction on the natural dimension from the high-recognition prescription. This falsifies a blanket 'more natural/representational logos always win' claim.
confirmed.quote: Low-investment logos (false sense of knowing and positive affect) should be less natural and very harmonious.

confirmed.claim: More descriptive logos — those whose textual and/or visual design elements clearly express the product or service category a brand markets — positively influence brand evaluations, purchase intentions, and brand performance, relative to less descriptive logos. This is a measured directional effect on brand equity, not just a preference.
confirmed.quote: more (vs. less) descriptive logos can positively influence brand evaluations, purchase intentions, and brand performance

confirmed.claim: The mechanism is processing fluency feeding perceived authenticity: descriptive logos are easier to process, which makes the brand seem more authentic, and consumers value that authenticity. The effect is mediated through fluency-driven authenticity rather than being a direct effect of descriptiveness itself.
confirmed.quote: These effects occur because more (vs. less) descriptive logos are easier to process and thus elicit stronger impressions of authenticity, which consumers value.

confirmed.claim: A descriptive logo does NOT always win. The benefit is considerably attenuated for brands already familiar to consumers, and it flips to a NEGATIVE effect for brands in product/service categories carrying negatively valenced associations. This directly falsifies any 'descriptive logo is always better' rule and supplies the boundary conditions a branding skill must encode.
confirmed.quote: the positive effects of logo descriptiveness are considerably attenuated for brands that are familiar (vs. unfamiliar) to consumers and reversed (i.e., negative) for brands that market a type of product/service linked with negatively (vs. positively) valenced associations in consumers' minds

confirmed.claim: Asymmetrical logos are perceived as more arousing than symmetrical logos, which raises perceptions of brand excitement — establishing logo (a)symmetry as a measurable visual dimension with a directional affective effect.
confirmed.quote: compared with symmetrical logos, asymmetrical logos tend to be more arousing, leading to increased perceptions of excitement.

confirmed.claim: Because asymmetrical logos read as more congruent with brands that have an 'exciting' personality, that design–personality fit boosts brand equity — not only consumer evaluations but also the market's financial valuations of the brand (the 'visual asymmetry effect').
confirmed.quote: consumers tend to perceive asymmetrical logos as more congruent with brands that have an exciting personality. This can boost consumers' evaluations and the market's financial valuations of such brands, a phenomenon referred to as the "visual asymmetry effect."

confirmed.claim: The benefit is conditional, not universal: the logo-design × brand-personality interaction holds ONLY for the excitement personality and ONLY for the visual property of asymmetry — so 'more symmetry is always better' is false, and asymmetry helps specifically for exciting brands.
confirmed.quote: The studies also show that this interplay between brand personality and logo design occurs only for the personality of excitement and the visual property of asymmetry.

confirmed.claim: Across three studies, incomplete typeface logos (where parts of the characters in the company name are intentionally missing/blanked out) produce a measured trade-off: they LOWER perceived firm trustworthiness but RAISE perceived firm innovativeness.
confirmed.quote: Three studies demonstrate that although incomplete typeface logos have an unfavorable influence on perceived firm trustworthiness, they have a favorable influence on perceived firm innovativeness.

confirmed.claim: The two effects run through distinct perceptual mechanisms: the trustworthiness penalty is mediated by the logo's perceived (lack of) clarity, while the innovativeness boost is mediated by the logo's perceived interestingness.
confirmed.quote: The former influence is tied to the logo's perceived clarity, while the latter influence is tied to its perceived interestingness.

confirmed.claim: Brand commitment is a NEGATIVE moderator of logo-redesign response: across two studies, higher brand commitment produced more negative evaluation of an inconsistent (angular-to-rounded) redesigned logo, and that negative logo evaluation mediated the downstream hit to brand attitude. (Redesign is not free; a brand's most committed customers punish a logo change the hardest.)
confirmed.quote: In two studies the authors show that brand commitment negatively influences evaluation of inconsistent information (i.e. rounded logo), and this negative logo evaluation mediates the impact on brand attitude.

confirmed.claim: Consumer response to an angular-to-rounded logo redesign is CONTINGENT (on brand commitment and self-construal), not unconditional. The mechanism studied is brand inconsistency from changing the logo, not an inherent superiority of rounded shapes — counter-evidence to any blanket 'rounded logos always win' claim.
confirmed.quote: This research aims to explore how consumer responses to logo redesign (from angular to rounded) are contingent on brand commitment and self‐construal.

confirmed.claim: Processing fluency is NOT a direct function of exposure count; a logo's visual complexity ('stimulus characteristics') together with presentation factors (repetition and spacing) interact with repetition to determine how much fluency a given level of exposure produces. This makes logo complexity a measured design moderator, refuting the naive 'more exposure always equals more liking' model.
confirmed.quote: we propose that stimulus characteristics and presentation factors will interact with repetition to determine the amount of processing fluency associated with a stimulus at various levels of exposure.

confirmed.claim: Hue maps onto specific brand-personality dimensions in a directional, experimentally-tested way: red evokes excitement/arousal, while blue conveys competence and trust (Study 1). This is the canonical empirical basis for color->brand-personality mapping. [Provenance: publisher abstract + search-indexed findings; primary PDF was paywalled and could not be read in full.]
confirmed.quote: While multiple shades of red evoke feelings of excitement and urgency, blue is typically associated with feelings of trust and competence... map hues onto brand personality dimensions (Study 1)

refuted.claim: The exposure-to-preference effect for brand names and packages operates through processing fluency and its MISATTRIBUTION at judgment time, not through a direct affective response to repetition. This identifies the causal mechanism (fluency, not raw familiarity) that an evidence-based branding skill must invoke when justifying logo/identity design choices.

Saturation and value (lightness) -- not hue alone -- systematically shift perceived brand personality: highly saturated colors map to excitement, competence and ruggedness, whereas low-saturation colors map to sincerity and sophistication (Study 2). This means 'blue = competent' is incomplete without specifying saturation/value, a key qualifier for any codified color rule. [Provenance: search-indexed abstract + citing literature; primary PDF paywalled.]

The review's core theoretical contribution is a two-part framework for how brand color conveys meaning: 'embodied' meaning, which is intrinsic to the color stimulus and context-independent, versus 'referential' meaning, which is learned semantic/cultural association. This separates a universal/biological color channel from a learned/associative one for brand-identity design.

Color contributes to brand recognition, but the peer-reviewed framing is conditional ('can'), tied to a specific cited study (Skorinko et al. 2006), and positioned as one component of 'visual equity' alongside image and likability — i.e. it supports a qualified recognition benefit, not an inflated universal figure such as the folkloric '80%' claim.

Brand color is multidimensional rather than just 'hue': all three color components (hue, saturation/chroma, value/lightness) influence perceived brand personality, and logo color together with shape influences brand likability — so color choices for identity should be specified on all three dimensions, not hue alone.

Logo color appropriateness is an INTERACTIVE (congruity) effect, not an absolute property of a color: functional products are judged more appropriate in 'functional' colors and sensory-social products more appropriate in 'sensory-social' colors (Experiment 1). This supports a congruence-based color-selection rule and undercuts any 'color X is always right' myth.

Color appropriateness is contingent on the brand's intended positioning: when consumers are aware of how a brand is trying to position itself, they judge colors congruent with that position as more appropriate (Experiment 2). Brand-position-color congruence is therefore a measurable moderator, not a fixed mapping.

Color differentiation from category norms is NOT universally beneficial to brand performance: across the analyzed categories it helped some and harmed others, falsifying the folklore that standing out via distinct brand color always wins.

Conforming to (rather than breaking from) established category color norms tends to benefit brands specifically in product categories that contain a dominant market leader and that are high-involvement.

Black-and-white logos increase perceived brand status relative to color logos (Yue & Xia 2026, 6 studies, N=2274). This is a falsifiable, directional logo-color effect on a brand-equity outcome (status), squarely in the brand-DESIGN scope.
